rationale: The page provides detailed information on crocodile conservation efforts in India, including specific sanctuaries, recovery statistics, and ongoing projects. It also features a comprehensive table outlining the characteristics and habitats of Saltwater, Marsh, and Gharial crocodiles found in India. This content is highly informative and suitable for generating multiple questions.
cleanedText:
Tikarpada (Satkosia) for gharials; Dangamal (Bhitarkanika) for saltwater crocodiles; Ramatirtha (Similipal) for mugger crocodiles.
Saltwater Crocodile & Mugger Recovery:
Saltwater crocodile population: Recovered to ~2,500 individuals
Mugger crocodile population: Rebounded to 8,000–10,000 individuals, reclaiming most of its historical range (i.e., Ganga River drainage).
Gharial Conservation: National Chambal Sanctuary (spanning Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh), Katarnia Ghat Wildlife Sanctuary (Uttar Pradesh), Gandak River, Corbett Tiger Reserve (Uttarakhand), and Son Gharial Sanctuary (Madhya Pradesh).
India now holds 80% of the global wild gharial population.
Ongoing Conservation Efforts:
Gharial Conservation Project (2025), aimed to expand gharial populations across the Ganges, Brahmaputra, and Indus rivers, as well as Mahanadi in Odisha.
Madras Crocodile Bank: Breeding crocodiles and supporting reintroduction programmes.
Crocodiles Found in India
Saltwater Crocodile (Estuarine Crocodile) (Crocodylus porosus)
Characteristics
Nocturnal animals.
Poikilothermic (regulate their body temperature only to a limited degree).
Diversity: 13 different crocodile species, 3 native species found in IndiaSaltwater crocodile.
Largest crocodile and Earth's largest living vertebrate-class reptile.
Strongest bite force of any living animal.
Muggers
Known to use 'tools' e.g. balance stick on their head to lure birds for nesting material.
Only Indian species that digs burrows on land to escape extreme temperatures.
Gharial
Adult males have a bulb-like structure on snout tip called GHARA (acts as a resonator to produce loud buzzing noises and blow bubbles to attract females).
Expert fish catchers.
Thinnest and most elongated snout among crocodilians.
Only visibly sexually dimorphic crocodilian.
Marsh Crocodile (Muggar, Broad-snouted Crocodile) (Crocodylus palustris)
Gharial (Fish Eating Crocodile, Indian Gavial, Gavial, Long Nosed Crocodile) (Gavialis gangeticus)
Habitat
Saltwater crocodile: Brackish water, mangrove swamps, estuaries, and coastal waters.
Bhitarkanika in Odisha (largest share), Andaman & Nicobar Islands and Sundarbans (West Bengal).
Muggers: Freshwater habitats like lakes, rivers, and slow-moving streams.
Gharial: Deep, clear, fast-flowing freshwater rivers with high sandbanks for nesting.
Nepal: Rapti-Narayan River.
India (Ganges tributaries): Girwa (UP), Son (MP), Ramganga (Uttarakhand), Gandak (Bihar), Chambal (UP, MP, Rajasthan), Mahanadi (Odisha).
